6.1.label: Question 6.1
6.1.synopsis: Type of product, method of distribution, target market, range and size of benefits, cost vs benefits of underwriting, feedback from customers and sellers, level of competition, approach of other insurers, reinsurers' views, regulatory constraints, technological advances including genetic testing
6.1.question: State the factors that will influence the approach to underwriting a health insurance product.

Key factors influencing the approach to: target market (eg socio-economic factors, age, financial sophistication)
Other influences: the approach taken by other insurers
regulatory constraints (eg the effect of the Disability Discrimination Acts – see Chapter 10)
impact of technological advances, such as genetic testing, and any constraints on the use of these.

6.2.label: Question 6.2
6.2.synopsis: Healthier population and lower claims, more accurate underwriting using genetic test results, design products for those with adverse results, cheaper rates for negative test results, offer genetic testing as part of a product
6.2.question: Suggest some opportunities that advances in genetic testing might offer a health insurer.

Opportunities from genetic testing: Genetic testing should, in general, help us to understand, detect, treat and prevent disease and so, in theory, we should end up with a healthier population and lower claims!

The opportunity to underwrite more accurately, using additional information provided through use of genetic test results (where permitted). In future, this is likely to be more accurate than using family history (or at the very least, a useful supplement).

Certain insurers could take advantage of the changes in technology to design products that meet the needs of certain customers better. For example, they may specialise in cover for people who have had adverse test results (and so perhaps cannot obtain cover elsewhere).
Insurers could offer cheaper rates to people who have had genetic tests that rule out predisposition towards certain diseases (ie negative test results).
Insurers could offer genetic testing (plus follow up and counselling, if necessary) as part of a product (eg as part of an employee benefit package).

6.3.label: Question 6.3
6.3.scenario: Describe how the market for IP insurance in a developed country with comprehensive State healthcare might be influenced by future:

(a).synopsis: Ageing population: longevity, retirement age, employment proportion falls, labour shortage, wage inflation, employer benefit reductions, more demand for State benefits but fewer taxpayers, perceived risk increases, IP market size may fall but demand may rise. Smaller family sizes: marrying/children later, fewer dependants reduces need, but more disposable income increases affordability
(a).question: demographic changes – an ageing population and reducing family sizes

Ageing population: Increasing longevity will mean that, unless retirement ages increase at a similar rate, the proportion of the population in employment will fall. [½]
This may result in lower levels of unemployment and shortages of labour in some sectors of the economy, which may increase the rate of wage inflation. [½]
As a result, employers may reduce benefits they provide to employees, eg less generous sick pay and occupational pension schemes. [½]

More demand for any State provided benefits (as people are living longer) together with a reduced number of taxpayers means that, unless taxes can be increased, these benefits may have to become less generous in the future. [1]
This will increase the perceived risk associated with becoming unable to work. [½]
While the overall numbers in employment and thus the potential size of the IP market may fall, the numbers at risk of loss of income as a result of long-term sickness or injury may rise, ... [½]
... because of less generous State and employer benefits, hence demand for IP may increase. [½]
Smaller family sizes: People are getting married and/or having children later in life, when they are more likely to have savings or be more able financially to look after them. [½]
With fewer dependants, there is less need for IP, hence reducing demand. [½]
However, with more disposable income, people may be more able to afford IP, hence leading to increase sales. [½]

(b).synopsis: Medical advances may eliminate or reduce disability length but increase long-term incapacity, difficult to determine net impact, insurers respond with wider margins or reviewable terms, significant cures could reduce perceived need, genetics may lead to adverse selection if insurers cannot use test results
(b).question: medical advances

Medical advances: While some medical advances may eliminate or reduce the length of certain disabilities, ... [½]
... there may be an increase in claims for long-term incapacity as a result of diseases that would previously have led to death. [½]
The longer-term impact on IP claims experience is therefore difficult to determine and will depend on the particular treatments that will be discovered (and these are unknown). [½]

If the impact of medical advances on IP claims experience becomes more uncertain, insurers may respond to this with increased margins in premiums or policies with reviewable, rather than guaranteed, premiums and terms. [1]

Arguably, significant or well-publicised medical cures might reduce the perceived need for IP insurance, for example, if a cure for 'bad backs' were found. Insurers would need to respond to such advances, eg with cheaper policies that don't cover back conditions. [1]
Advances in genetics may lead to increased selection against the insurers, particularly if it becomes possible to detect susceptibility to long-term debilitating diseases, eg multiple sclerosis. [1]
If insurers cannot use the results of genetic tests to underwrite, the market could be affected by increased margins in premiums or, at the extreme, insurers pulling out of the market. [1]

(c).synopsis: Financial wealth increases awareness and claims consciousness, disposable income levels, future inflation expectations affect product type purchased, buoyant economy reduces reluctance to return to work, greater wealth and mobility increases self-employment and individual IP demand
(c).question: economic changes

Economic changes: Increased financial wealth may lead to increased financial awareness, resulting in a greater awareness of the need for IP (thus increasing demand) but also a greater claims consciousness (thus more claims). [1]
Also, the levels of disposable income people have affects the demand for IP insurance, particularly among those who do not see it as a necessary purchase. [½]

Expectations as regards future inflation may influence the type of product that is purchased. If future inflation is very uncertain or if high levels of inflation are expected, policies with increasing benefits, particularly if the increases are linked to inflation, will be more popular. [1]
If the economy is buoyant, people are generally less reluctant to return to work. This improves claims costs and hence reduces premiums on new and reviewable policies. [½]

Economic factors, such as having greater wealth or greater mobility of work, may enable more people to run their own businesses. This will increase the demand for individual IP, which mainly comes from the self-employed. [1]

(d).synopsis: State benefit level influences demand, restrictions on State benefits, changes in State benefits increase risk awareness, tax incentives for self-employment increase individual IP demand, direct taxation reductions increase disposable income, government encouragement of private IP, State-insurer collaboration on healthy lifestyles, radical political changes affect product design
(d).question: political changes

Political changes: The level of State sickness and disability benefits may influence demand for IP insurance. For example, an erosion of the level of State benefits that are linked to price inflation (rather than earnings inflation) may prompt some people to purchase IP. [1]
Any restrictions on the availability of State sickness and disability benefits may similarly influence demand – for example, moves towards the means testing of benefits. [½]

Any change in State sickness or disability benefits, particularly if a detrimental one, may increase awareness of risks associated with long-term sickness and thus increase the demand for IP insurance. [½]
Political measures, such as tax incentives, may also encourage people to become self-employed, increasing demand for individual IP (as mentioned above). [½]
A reduction in direct taxation will increase consumers' disposable income and may increase the demand for IP insurance. [½]

A big factor is whether the government wishes to encourage people to purchase private IP insurance. If it does, the incentives or publicity it uses to do so will act to increase demand for the products. This may also increase competition in the market. [1]

The State may wish to work with the IP insurance industry in encouraging healthy lifestyles, safe working practices and effective rehabilitation when incapacitated. This would benefit both the State (in paying less benefits) and insurers (in less claims). [1]
Any radical political changes – for example, to State benefits or to the regulation of IP insurance – will cause insurers to respond, eg by changing product designs. [½]

(e).synopsis: Virus affecting working age leads to higher claim rates, longer illness duration increases claims, claims management more difficult, high mortality may limit claims if death within deferred period, overwhelmed State healthcare increases claim rates, economic recession reduces volumes and increases lapses, insurer's own operations affected, regulator stress testing and capital requirements, asset value falls
(e).question: pandemics.

Pandemics: The exact impact will depend on the impacts of the virus. An IP insurance product would be more impacted by a virus that affects working age individuals when it would lead to higher claim rates. [1]
The virus could also increase the duration of claims if it leads to a long illness and recovery time. [½]
Claims management may be made more difficult by the pandemic, leading to less frequent checks and fewer in person visits. This could also increase the duration of claims. [½]
If the virus has a high mortality rate, claims may not increase as death may occur within the deferred period. [½]
It may potentially reduce overall claims durations if the virus kills people who are claiming for other reasons. [½]
If the State's healthcare system is overwhelmed due to the pandemic, claim rates could increase as individuals are unable to get treatment for other illnesses. [1]
The pandemic may have an impact on the economic environment in the country, leading to a recession. [½]
For group IP insurance, a recession would lead to lower new business volumes and smaller scheme sizes, as companies try to save money. [½]
A recession could also reduce new business volumes on individual IP insurance if individuals have reduced disposable income. [½]
The reduction in disposable income could also lead to increase lapses as IP insurance is often considered a 'luxury'. [½]
The pandemic could also have impacts on the insurer's own business. For example: [½]
• increased sickness level among its own staff could lead to admin delays [½]
• it may not be possible for staff to get to the insurer's office, setting up working from home for all staff may be time consuming and costly, [½]
The regulator may be concerned about insurance companies' ability to withstand the pandemic and require stress testing to be carried out and increase capital requirements. [1]
If there is an economic impact, this could also lead to a fall in the value of assets held by the insurer. [½]
